panel.title: Security Configuration Panel
panel.description: Toggle security features on and off to demonstrate the difference between secure and insecure implementations.
panel.mfaDesc: Require Google Authenticator TOTP for admin login
panel.rbacDesc: Restrict access based on user roles (student/professor/admin)
panel.passwordEncryptionDesc: Hash passwords with bcrypt instead of storing plaintext
panel.fieldEncryptionDesc: Encrypt sensitive data like SSN and grades
panel.httpsDesc: Secure communication with encryption in transit (requires server restart)
panel.auditLoggingDesc: Track all user actions and security events
panel.rateLimitingDesc: Protect against brute force login attempts
panel.sodDesc: Require admin approval for class deletion
panel.byokDesc: Use custom encryption key instead of default
panel.backupsDesc: Automatically backup database at regular intervals

warnings.encryptionActive: Please disable field encryption before changing encryption keys.

sod.title: Segregation of Duties

sod.workflowExplanation: This security feature requires administrative approval before classes can be deleted. This prevents unauthorized or accidental deletions and ensures proper oversight.
sod.step1: You submit a deletion request for this class
sod.step3: If approved, the class will be deleted along with all sessions and enrollments
sod.step4: If rejected, you will be notified with the reason
sod.deletionWarning: If your request is approved, this action cannot be undone. All class sessions, enrollments, and student grades will be permanently deleted.
sod.confirmSubmit: Are you sure you want to submit a deletion request for this class? An administrator will need to approve before deletion occurs.
sod.requestSubmitted: Deletion request submitted successfully! You will be notified when an administrator reviews your request.
sod.noPendingRequests: No pending deletion requests at this time.
sod.rejectPrompt: Please provide a reason for rejecting this deletion request. The professor will see this reason.
sod.reasonPlaceholder: Example: This class is still active and has enrolled students. Please wait until the semester ends.
sod.confirmApprove: Are you sure you want to APPROVE this deletion request? The class and all related data will be permanently deleted.
sod.approvalSuccess: Deletion request approved. Class has been deleted.
sod.reasonRequired: Please provide a reason for rejection.

backups.title: Database Backups
backups.noBackups: No backups found. Create your first backup above.
backups.confirmRestore: This will restore the database to the state in this backup. Current data will be backed up first. Continue?
backups.confirmCleanup: This will delete all backups except the 50 most recent. Continue?

byok.title: Bring Your Own Key (BYOK)
byok.encryptionWarningText: Field encryption is currently ENABLED. You must disable field encryption in the Security Panel before uploading or deleting encryption keys to prevent data loss.
byok.instructions: Use OpenSSL to generate a secure 256-bit encryption key. Run the following command in your terminal:
byok.noteText: This will generate a random 32-byte key encoded in base64 format. Copy the entire output (e.g., "1vX9K7kJ2mN5pQ8rT3uV6wY0zA4bC7dE9fG2hI5jL8m=").
byok.practice1: 🔒 Always DISABLE field encryption before changing or deleting encryption keys
byok.practice2: 💾 Keep a secure backup of your custom key - if you lose it, encrypted data cannot be recovered
byok.practice3: 🔄 After uploading a new key, existing encrypted data remains encrypted with the OLD key until you decrypt and re-encrypt it
byok.practice4: ⚠️ Changing keys while data is encrypted will make that data permanently unrecoverable
byok.practice5: 📝 Document your key management procedures and keep keys in a secure location
byok.errorShortKey: Key must be at least 16 characters long
byok.confirmUpload: ⚠️ Are you sure you want to upload this custom encryption key? Make sure field encryption is disabled first!
byok.confirmDelete: ⚠️ Are you sure you want to delete the custom key and revert to the default key? Make sure field encryption is disabled first!
byok.deleteSuccess: Custom key deleted successfully. Reverted to default key.

studentLab.title: Static Code Analysis Lab
studentLab.subtitle: Review each finding, classify it, and document your reasoning — then submit when you are ready.
studentLab.completionMessage: You have reviewed and submitted all 12 findings. Well done!

findingDetail.notImported: This finding has not yet been imported to the vulnerability manager.

instructor.title: Static Code Analysis — Instructor Dashboard
instructor.confirmImport: Push this finding to the Vulnerability Manager?

guided.introBannerText: For each finding, read the code, identify whether the vulnerability is real, and document your reasoning. There are no wrong answers — what matters is the quality of your analysis.

1.title: Hardcoded Session Secret
1.description: The Express session secret is hardcoded in source code. Anyone with code access can forge session cookies, leading to authentication bypass. In this application, this could compromise all active sessions, allowing an attacker to impersonate any student or professor and access their grades or enrollment data.
1.remediation: Move the secret to an environment variable (SESSION_SECRET). Generate a cryptographically random 64-byte value for production.
1.hint1: What happens if an attacker gains access to the source code?
1.hint2: Should this value be in the code or in the environment?
1.hint3: What kind of data could an attacker forge with this secret?

2.title: Hardcoded AES Encryption Key
2.description: The AES-256 encryption key is hardcoded. Compromise of the key allows decryption of all encrypted PII (SSNs, grades) in the database. In this application, an attacker could decrypt the social security numbers and grades of all enrolled students, causing a massive personal data breach.
2.remediation: Load the key from an environment variable (ENCRYPTION_KEY). Use a key derivation function (PBKDF2/Argon2) if deriving from a passphrase.
2.hint1: If someone obtains this key, what data can they decrypt?
2.hint2: How should cryptographic secrets be managed in a production application?
2.hint3: What is the difference between storing a key in code versus in an environment variable?

3.title: Plaintext Credentials Logged to Console
3.description: Plaintext passwords are written to the console/log on every login attempt. Log aggregation systems (Splunk, CloudWatch) will store and expose credentials. In this application, every login attempt by a student or professor logs their password in plaintext, which could be exploited by anyone with access to server logs.
3.remediation: Remove the password from the log statement. Log only the username and outcome (success/failure).
3.hint1: What information is visible in the server logs?
3.hint2: Who might have access to log files in production?
3.hint3: What information is safe to log during a login attempt?

4.title: Plaintext Password Comparison
4.description: Passwords are stored and compared in plaintext. A database breach exposes every user's password immediately. In this application, if the database is compromised, passwords for all students and professors would be instantly readable, allowing impersonation of any account.
4.remediation: Hash passwords with bcrypt/argon2 on registration. Compare using bcrypt.compare() at login. Migrate existing users on next login.
4.hint1: How are passwords stored in the database?
4.hint2: What would an attacker see if they obtained a copy of the database?
4.hint3: What technique allows verifying a password without storing it in plaintext?

5.title: Audit Logging Defaults to OFF
5.description: Audit logging is disabled by default. Security-relevant events (logins, privilege changes, data access) are not recorded, preventing incident detection and forensics. In this application, if a student accessed another student's grades or if an account was compromised, there would be no trail to detect or investigate the incident.
5.remediation: Change the default to audit_logging: 1. Document the intentional-off toggle in the Security Panel as a teaching tool only.
5.hint1: What happens when a security feature is disabled by default rather than enabled?
5.hint2: How could you detect unauthorized access without audit logs?
5.hint3: What security principle is violated by an insecure default setting?

6.title: IDOR: No Ownership Check on Enrollment Access
6.description: The endpoint uses the student ID from the URL path rather than the authenticated session. Students can read any other student's enrollment records by changing the ID in the URL. In this application, a student could view the grades, enrollments, and academic record of any other student simply by changing a number in the URL.
6.remediation: Replace req.params.studentId with req.session.userId. Verify resource ownership before returning data.
6.hint1: Where does the student ID come from in this request — the session or the URL?
6.hint2: What happens if a student manually changes the ID in the URL?
6.hint3: How should the server verify that the user has the right to access this resource?

7.title: No CSRF Protection on State-Changing Requests
7.description: POST/PUT/DELETE routes have no CSRF token validation. An attacker can craft a malicious page that triggers authenticated state-changing actions on behalf of a logged-in user. In this application, an attacker could submit code analysis reviews on behalf of a student or modify courses on behalf of a professor through a crafted link.
7.remediation: Add the csurf middleware (or a modern equivalent like csrf-csrf). Include the token in all forms and AJAX requests.
7.hint1: What prevents an external site from submitting a form to this application?
7.hint2: How can the server distinguish a legitimate request from a forged one?
7.hint3: Which actions in this application modify data and should be protected?

8.title: Rate Limiting Only on Login Route
8.description: Rate limiting is only applied to the login endpoint. Password reset, MFA verification, and API endpoints remain vulnerable to automated brute-force attacks. In this application, an attacker could perform unlimited attempts on MFA verification or submit massive API requests without restriction.
8.remediation: Apply rate limiting globally or to all sensitive endpoints. Use different limits per endpoint based on sensitivity.
8.hint1: Is the login route the only endpoint sensitive to brute-force attacks?
8.hint2: What other endpoints could benefit from rate limiting?
8.hint3: How could an attacker exploit the absence of rate limiting on API endpoints?

9.title: No HTTP Security Headers
9.description: The application does not set security headers (CSP, X-Frame-Options, HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options). This leaves it vulnerable to clickjacking, MIME-sniffing, and XSS amplification. In this application, an attacker could embed the login page in a malicious iframe to capture student and professor credentials.
9.remediation: Add the helmet middleware: app.use(helmet()). Configure Content-Security-Policy for the application's specific sources.
9.hint1: What types of attacks do HTTP security headers prevent?
9.hint2: What happens if this application is loaded in an iframe on a malicious site?
9.hint3: What popular Node.js middleware automatically configures these headers?

10.title: Path Traversal in Backup Download
10.description: The backup filename comes directly from the URL without validation. An attacker with admin access can request ../../etc/passwd or any other file on the server. In this application, a malicious administrator or compromised admin account could download the complete database or configuration files containing application secrets.
10.remediation: Validate that the resolved path starts with BACKUP_DIR: assert(filepath.startsWith(BACKUP_DIR)). Reject requests with path separators in the filename.
10.hint1: Is the filename validated before being used to read a file on the server?
10.hint2: What happens if the filename contains sequences like '../'?
10.hint3: How can you ensure the resolved path stays within the intended directory?

11.title: Outdated express-session with Known Vulnerabilities
11.description: The project uses an older version of express-session. Dependencies should be kept current to include security patches. In this application, a known vulnerability in session management could allow an attacker to hijack active sessions of students and professors.
11.remediation: Run npm audit fix. Pin to the latest stable version. Add npm audit to the CI pipeline to catch regressions.
11.hint1: How can you check if project dependencies contain known vulnerabilities?
11.hint2: What risk does an outdated dependency pose even if the application code is correct?
11.hint3: What npm tool identifies and automatically fixes dependency vulnerabilities?

12.title: Session Cookie Missing secure Flag
12.description: The session cookie does not have the secure flag set. Over an HTTP connection the session token is transmitted in plaintext and can be intercepted by a network attacker. In this application, on a shared Wi-Fi network like the university's, an attacker could intercept session cookies and impersonate any logged-in user.
12.remediation: Set secure: process.env.NODE_ENV === "production" so the flag is active in production HTTPS deployments while remaining usable in local HTTP dev.
12.hint1: What does a session cookie contain and why must it be protected?
12.hint2: On an unencrypted network, who can see cookies transmitted over HTTP?
12.hint3: What cookie flag prevents transmission over an insecure connection?

1.title: IDOR: Access Another Student's Grades
1.description: When RBAC is disabled the server skips ownership checks. A student can view any other student's grades by modifying the student ID in the URL.
steps: Log in as alice_student (password: student123)
Navigate to My Classes and note your student ID in the URL
Go to /classes and find a class you are not enrolled in
Manually change the student ID in the URL to another student's ID (e.g., try IDs 1-8)
Observe that you can read enrolment records and grades belonging to other students
Document the affected URL, the IDs you were able to access, and the data exposed
1.expected_finding: Student can read grade records belonging to other users without authorisation

2.title: Plaintext Password Storage in Database
2.description: Passwords are stored in plaintext in the JSON database. Anyone who can read the database file — or trigger a backup download — obtains every user's password.
steps: Log in as admin (password: admin123)
Go to Admin → Backup & Restore
Click "Create Backup Now" then download the backup file
Open the JSON file and locate the users array
Observe that the password field contains the plaintext password for every user
Note that you can now log in as any user without needing to crack any hash
2.expected_finding: Plaintext passwords visible in the database backup

3.title: CSRF: Force Admin to Disable Security Feature
3.description: State-changing POST requests do not include CSRF tokens. An attacker can craft a page that, when visited by a logged-in admin, silently disables a security setting.
steps: Log in as admin and note that Audit Logging is currently enabled
Open browser DevTools → Network tab
Toggle Audit Logging off and on, capture the POST request
Craft an HTML page with a hidden form that submits the same POST to the same URL
Send the link to the admin (or open it in a second tab while still logged in as admin)
Observe that the security setting changes without any CSRF token being required
3.expected_finding: Security settings can be changed by a forged cross-origin request

4.title: Brute Force MFA Verification (No Rate Limit)
4.description: The MFA token verification endpoint does not enforce rate limiting. An attacker can automate requests to guess a 6-digit TOTP code (1 000 000 possible values) within seconds.
steps: Enable MFA for alice_student from the profile page
Log out, then attempt to log in as alice_student
At the MFA prompt, intercept the POST /auth/verify-mfa request with Burp Suite or a script
Send 1000 sequential 6-digit codes via repeater/intruder in under 60 seconds
Observe that none of the requests return a 429 Too Many Requests response
Note that a real attacker could exhaust the full keyspace in ~17 minutes
4.expected_finding: MFA verification accepts unlimited attempts with no lockout

5.title: Credentials Exposed in Server Logs
5.description: The server logs the plaintext username and password on every login attempt. The logs are accessible in the admin panel.
steps: Log in as admin and open the Audit Logs page
In a second tab, attempt to log in as any user (successful or failed)
Return to Audit Logs and observe the new entry
If you have server console access, inspect the console output for the login attempt
Note the plaintext password visible in the console/log entry
Consider the impact if these logs are shipped to a centralised log system
5.expected_finding: User passwords appear in plaintext in application logs

6.title: Path Traversal: Read Arbitrary Server Files via Backup Download
6.description: The backup download endpoint uses the filename from the URL without sanitisation. An admin (or attacker with admin access) can request any file on the server.
steps: Log in as admin
Navigate to Admin → Backup & Restore and create at least one backup
Note the backup filename format: backup-YYYY-MM-DD-HHMMSS.json
Modify the download URL, replacing the filename with: ../../package.json
Observe that the server returns the contents of package.json
Try ../../.env or ../../config/database.js to see what other files are readable
6.expected_finding: Path traversal allows reading arbitrary server files

studentLab.subtitle: Follow each scenario step-by-step, exploit the vulnerability, then document your findings.

form.triggeredQuestion: Did you successfully trigger the vulnerability?

precondition.none: No precondition required — scenario is always available.
precondition.rbacMet: RBAC is OFF — scenario is unlocked.
precondition.rbacUnmet: Requires RBAC to be disabled. Ask your instructor to toggle it in the Security Panel.
precondition.rateLimitMet: Rate limiting is OFF — scenario is unlocked.
precondition.rateLimitUnmet: Requires Rate Limiting to be disabled. Ask your instructor to toggle it in the Security Panel.
